comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the legality and ethics of reverse-engineering Viktor and making it open source, with some commenters emphasizing the ease of replicating such tools and others focusing on the potential legal ramifications. There is also commentary on the nature of the product and the motivations behind the reverse-engineering effort.

comments.author: redfloatplane
comments.text:
There are gonna be some really interesting legal decisions to read in the coming years, that’s for sure…

---

The rest of this comment is irrelevant, but leaving for posterity, I had the wrong Viktor - it's getviktor.com not viktor.ai:

Edit: this one particularly interesting to me as both parties are in the EU. VIKTOR.ai is a Dutch company and the author of this post is Polish.

The ToS for Viktor.ai include the following fun passages:

> 18.1. The Agreement and these Terms & Conditions are governed by Dutch law and the Agreement and these Terms & Conditions will be interpreted in accordance with Dutch law.

18.2. All disputes arising from or arising in connection with the Agreement and/or the Terms & Conditions will be submitted exclusively to the competent court in Rotterdam, The Netherlands.

7.3. The Customer is not permitted to change, remove or make unrecognizable any mark showing VIKTOR's Intellectual Property Rights to the Software. The Customer is not permitted to use or register any trademark or design or any domain name of VIKTOR or a similar name or sign in any country.

8.5. The Customer may not cause or allow any reproduction, imitation, duplication, copying, sale, resale, leasing or trading of the Services and/or the Software, or any part thereof.
comments.analysis: This comment provides detailed and accurate information about the legal terms governing Viktor.ai, highlighting relevant clauses that impact the reverse-engineering discussion.

comments.author: empath75
comments.text:
I just want to point out _how easy it is_ to build any of these tools yourself.

I got inspired by nano-claw and built on some of it's ideas to build a whole k8s hosted autonomous agent platform and got it into production in 2 weeks. It's just some api calls and container orchestration. The only hard problem _and it is hard_, is securing it, because you basically have to treat the agents as potentially malicious.
comments.analysis: The comment offers a practical perspective on the technical feasibility of building similar tools, emphasizing security challenges as the main difficulty.

comments.author: hmokiguess
comments.text:
A lot of folks talking about the legality basis of this approach, I think the focus should be more around the true moat behind these hype growth hack companies out there.

This sort of exposure is good, in my opinion it enables competition to emerge with true business moat rather than just "do it first, do it fast, worry last" type of thing we're seeing.
comments.analysis: This comment shifts the focus from legality to business strategy, suggesting that competition should be based on sustainable advantages rather than speed or hype.

comments.author: yashasolutions
comments.text:
Nice. Probably worth making a local copy before it gets taken down.

(Re: legal - why even bother with a court decision when it’s on GitHub? A takedown is much simpler. We've seen this before, like when Meta went after people reverse-engineering their API)

That said there may not be much here thats actually protectable. It's mostly a CLI orchestrating other tools, and the same functionality could likely be reproduced fairly easily, especially with AI.

Still, props to him for writing a proper blog post and explaining the process
comments.analysis: The comment realistically assesses the technical and legal situation, noting the simplicity of the tool and the likelihood of takedown without court involvement.

comments.author: kklisura
comments.text: I find it fascinating that this is just some _plumbing_ around some LLM - sold as a product and "AI coworker". They don't own the LLM model. You're just one silent update from not delivering what you promised.
comments.analysis: This comment insightfully points out that the product relies on third-party LLMs, which may undermine the product's stability and uniqueness.

comments.author: klustregrif
comments.text: Ignorering the questionable legality of this entire thing, which might fall out in favor of it being a legal activity. Calling the project OpenViktor is as dumb as committing secrets to GitHub. For the love of reason call it something else.
comments.analysis: The comment warns about the risks of naming the project OpenViktor, implying potential legal or reputational issues, which is a reasonable concern.

comments.author: cubefox
comments.analysis: This brief comment expresses an ethical judgment without elaboration, reflecting a common but subjective viewpoint.

comments.author: dirk94018
comments.text: "Driven by curiosity and envy, I tested it out." Two days of work driven by envy. Not what open source should be about.
comments.analysis: The comment critiques the motivation behind the reverse-engineering effort, suggesting it conflicts with open source values, though this is subjective.

comments.author: popcorncowboy
comments.text: Every part of this is genuinely funny. Viktor launching basically OpenClaw for slack (because claw is MIT so yolo let's go mac some monnney and PJ outta Dubai etc etc). Some guy getting all their source code just by asking Viktor for it. Then rebuilding their core in a couple days because there's basically nothing there. push MIT to Github. TeamViktor's faces surely? But they're busy diamond handsing all the way to the moon so probably don't even care. XD
comments.analysis: This comment is sarcastic and somewhat mocking but contains plausible observations about the simplicity of the original product.

comments.author: petterroea
comments.text: Is this reverse-engineering or "just" data exfiltration
comments.analysis: The comment raises a valid question about the nature of the activity, though it does not provide further analysis.

comments.author: vixalien
comments.text: How did you make the image at https://github.com/zggf-zggf/openviktor/blob/main/docs/asset...?
comments.analysis: This comment is a simple question unrelated to the core discussion and adds little value.

comments.analysis: This comment appears to be a joke or trolling, contributing no substantive insight.
